title: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) Basics
content: CBT is a structured, goal-oriented form of psychotherapy that focuses on identifying and changing negative thought patterns. The core principle is that our thoughts, feelings, and behaviors are interconnected. By changing unhelpful thinking patterns, we can improve our emotional state and behavior. Key techniques include thought records, behavioral activation, and cognitive restructuring.

title: Mindfulness Meditation
content: Mindfulness is the practice of paying attention to the present moment without judgment. Research shows it reduces stress, anxiety, and depression. Start with 5 minutes daily: sit comfortably, focus on your breath, and gently return attention when your mind wanders. Body scan meditation and mindful walking are also effective practices.

title: Grounding Techniques for Anxiety
content: The 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique helps during anxiety or panic attacks. Name 5 things you can see, 4 things you can touch, 3 things you can hear, 2 things you can smell, and 1 thing you can taste. This redirects focus from anxious thoughts to the present sensory experience.

title: Sleep Hygiene Practices
content: Good sleep hygiene improves mental health significantly. Maintain a consistent sleep schedule, avoid screens 1 hour before bed, keep your bedroom cool and dark, limit caffeine after 2 PM, and create a relaxing bedtime routine. Adults need 7-9 hours of quality sleep per night.

title: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
content: PMR involves systematically tensing and releasing muscle groups to reduce physical tension and anxiety. Start from your toes, tense each muscle group for 5 seconds, then release for 30 seconds. Work upward through legs, abdomen, chest, arms, and face. Practice for 10-15 minutes daily.

title: Journaling for Mental Health
content: Expressive writing helps process emotions, reduce stress, and gain self-awareness. Write freely for 15-20 minutes about your thoughts and feelings. Gratitude journaling (listing 3 things you're grateful for daily) has been shown to improve well-being and reduce depressive symptoms.

title: Building Social Connections
content: Social isolation significantly impacts mental health. Prioritize meaningful connections: schedule regular check-ins with friends, join community groups, volunteer, or participate in group activities. Even brief positive social interactions can boost mood and reduce feelings of loneliness.

title: Exercise and Mental Health
content: Regular physical activity is one of the most effective natural antidepressants. Aim for 150 minutes of moderate exercise weekly. Walking, swimming, yoga, and dancing all provide benefits. Exercise releases endorphins, reduces cortisol, improves sleep, and boosts self-esteem.

title: Managing Stress Effectively
content: Chronic stress affects both physical and mental health. Effective strategies include time management, setting boundaries, regular breaks, deep breathing exercises, and identifying stress triggers. The STOP technique: Stop, Take a breath, Observe your experience, Proceed mindfully.

title: Understanding Emotions
content: All emotions are valid and serve a purpose. Primary emotions include joy, sadness, anger, fear, surprise, and disgust. Learning to identify, name, and accept emotions is called emotional literacy. The feeling wheel can help expand your emotional vocabulary beyond 'good' or 'bad'.

title: Breathing Exercises for Calm
content: Box breathing: Inhale for 4 counts, hold for 4, exhale for 4, hold for 4. Repeat 4-6 times. The 4-7-8 technique: Inhale for 4 counts, hold for 7, exhale slowly for 8. These activate the parasympathetic nervous system, reducing the fight-or-flight response.

title: Setting Healthy Boundaries
content: Boundaries protect your mental health and relationships. Learn to say no without guilt, communicate your needs clearly, recognize signs of boundary violations, and prioritize self-care. Healthy boundaries are not selfish—they are necessary for sustainable well-being.

title: Dealing with Negative Self-Talk
content: Negative self-talk patterns include catastrophizing, black-and-white thinking, mind reading, and personalization. Challenge these thoughts by asking: Is this thought true? What evidence supports or contradicts it? What would I tell a friend in this situation? Replace with balanced, compassionate self-talk.

title: Crisis Resources and When to Seek Help
content: Seek professional help if you experience persistent sadness, hopelessness, inability to function, thoughts of self-harm, or substance abuse. In crisis, contact: National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (988), Crisis Text Line (text HOME to 741741), or call 911 for immediate danger. You are not alone.

title: Positive Psychology and Flourishing
content: Martin Seligman's PERMA model outlines five pillars of well-being: Positive Emotions, Engagement (flow), Relationships, Meaning, and Accomplishment. Incorporate activities aligned with each pillar. Character strengths identification and use is another key positive psychology practice.
